feature_name: GPIO pin banking (2x16) with up to 32 GPIO signals
description: The controller exposes up to 32 GPIO signals grouped into two banks of 16 pins each. Control registers are organized as 32-bit words addressing paired banks, with bit positions mapping directly to pin numbers. This banking simplifies simultaneous configuration and status reads. The mapping convention uses register_name01 and bit fields per pin. The structure supports scalable per-pin control while enabling bank-level operations.
dependencies: Pin multiplexing configuration; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Use Table 1 to map each GP[x] to its bank/bit when building register masks.

feature_name: Pin multiplexing control for GPIO function selection
description: GPIO pins share package balls with other peripheral functions and require pin-mux configuration. Multiplexing is set by a combination of hardware configuration at reset and software-programmable registers. Correct muxing is a prerequisite before GPIO registers affect external pins. Device-specific data manual must be consulted for exact mux settings. Misconfiguration will leave pins under alternate peripheral control.
dependencies: Device-specific PINMUX registers; Boot-time strap; PSC enable
Remarks: Apply pin-mux early in initialization before setting DIR/OUT registers.

feature_name: Clock gating via PSC (input clock CPU/6)
description: The GPIO peripheral clock is disabled by default and must be enabled through the local Power and Sleep Controller (PSC). Once enabled, the GPIO module clock runs at CPU/6. Without clock enable, register accesses will not function and interrupts/events will not be generated. Power sequencing must respect PSC procedures. Clock control impacts interrupt edge synchronization and input sampling.
dependencies: PSC module for GPIO; System clock configuration
Remarks: Gate the clock only after pin-muxing and before functional configuration to avoid spurious edges.

feature_name: Endianness independence of GPIO operation
description: GPIO logic and register semantics are independent of CPU endianness. Software can use the same bit positions and fields in either endian mode. This reduces portability concerns across builds. No byte-lane swapping is applied within the GPIO register block. Behavior is identical for LE and BE configurations.
dependencies: NONE
Remarks: Common driver code can be shared across endian builds without conditional handling.

feature_name: Per-pin direction control with default input state
description: Each GPIO pin direction is controlled per-bit using DIR01, with 1 configuring input and 0 configuring output. All pins default to inputs after reset. Outputs only drive when DIRn is cleared. Reads to DIR provide current configuration and should be used to avoid unintended drive conflicts. Direction settings apply independently to both banks.
dependencies: PSC clock enable; Pin multiplexing
Remarks: Always set DIR before changing output data to prevent glitches.

feature_name: Output control via SET_DATA, CLR_DATA, and OUT_DATA
description: Outputs can be driven high using SET_DATA01 and low using CLR_DATA01, enabling atomic set/clear without read-modify-write. OUT_DATA01 provides a combined write/read view for bulk updates and reflects the register value, not necessarily the external pin. Using separate set/clear allows multiple software contexts to manipulate bits without critical sections. Writes to output registers have no effect on pins still configured as inputs. Read-backs should use IN_DATA01 to observe actual external level when needed.
dependencies: DIR01 configured for output; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Prefer SET/CLR for concurrency; reserve OUT_DATA read-modify-write for controlled single-context updates.

feature_name: Input sampling via IN_DATA with clock-domain synchronization
description: IN_DATA01 returns the synchronized state of input pins to the GPIO clock. For pins configured as outputs, IN_DATA01 returns the device-driven level on the pad. This ensures clean sampling aligned with the module clock domain. For open-drain or wired-logic scenarios, IN_DATA01 reports the resolved external level. Use this register for debouncing in software if required.
dependencies: PSC clock enable; External pull-ups/pull-downs as applicable
Remarks: Poll IN_DATA01 for status or use interrupts for edge-driven behavior depending on latency needs.

feature_name: Per-bank interrupt/event enable (BINTEN)
description: Interrupts and EDMA events from GPIO are enabled per bank via BINTEN bits EN0 and EN1. Enabling a bank permits all its pins to act as interrupt sources to the DSP CPU. Disabling a bank masks both interrupt and EDMA event signaling. Bank mapping corresponds to pins 0–15 and 16–31 respectively. Additional CPU-level routing is device-specific.
dependencies: CPU interrupt controller configuration; EDMA event mapping; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Enable only the banks in use to reduce unintended wakeups and ISR load.

feature_name: Configurable edge triggering per pin (rise, fall, both)
description: Each pin supports independent edge selection using SET_RIS_TRIG/CLR_RIS_TRIG and SET_FAL_TRIG/CLR_FAL_TRIG. Rising-only, falling-only, both edges, or disabled can be programmed per bit. Edge detection is synchronized to the GPIO module clock to avoid metastability. Direction does not need to be input for edge event generation, enabling software-driven test interrupts. Proper pairing of set and clear operations defines the final edge configuration.
dependencies: BINTEN enable for the bank; CPU interrupt controller; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Write the complementary CLR register when selecting a single edge to avoid unintended both-edge configuration.

feature_name: Interrupt status with write-1-to-clear (W1C)
description: Pending GPIO interrupts are indicated per pin in INTSTAT01 with a logic 1. Software clears a latched interrupt by writing a 1 back to the corresponding STAT bit; writing 0 has no effect. For direct CPU-routed GPIO interrupts, CPU-side flags may also be read. For bank interrupts, software must consult INTSTAT01 to identify the source pin. Robust ISRs should read, service, and W1C clear all asserted bits.
dependencies: CPU ISR; BINTEN enable; Edge trigger configuration
Remarks: Read-modify-write is not required for W1C fields; write only the bits to be cleared to avoid disturbing others.

feature_name: GPIO events as EDMA synchronization sources
description: All GPIO signals can generate events that synchronize the Enhanced DMA (EDMA) engine. This enables hardware-triggered transfers based on external edges. The enable control shares BINTEN bits with interrupt masking. Event-to-channel mapping details are device-specific and found in the data manual. Use edge selection to shape EDMA trigger behavior.
dependencies: EDMA channel mapping; BINTEN enable; Edge trigger configuration; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Coordinate interrupt and EDMA usage to avoid double-handling the same edge if both are enabled.

feature_name: Power management and low-power behavior via PSC
description: The PSC can place the GPIO peripheral into reduced-power states during inactivity. While in low power, interrupt/event generation is suspended, but output pins retain their last driven state. Restoring the module from low power resumes normal edge detection and sampling. Proper PSC sequencing is required to avoid glitches. Power policy should consider wake sources and bank enables.
dependencies: PSC power state transitions; BINTEN and edge config revalidation on resume
Remarks: Reinitialize or verify configuration after power cycling the peripheral to ensure expected behavior.

feature_name: Initialization sequence after hardware reset
description: After a hardware reset, software must: configure pin multiplexing, enable the GPIO module via PSC, and program direction, data, and interrupt control registers. Without these steps, the peripheral remains inactive or in default input state. The sequence ensures pins are under GPIO control and clocked. Order of operations prevents spurious transitions. This forms the baseline bring-up routine for the module.
dependencies: Device PINMUX registers; PSC; DIR/OUT/SET/CLR; Edge trigger and BINTEN
Remarks: Document and centralize GPIO init to avoid duplication across subsystems.

feature_name: Reset behavior: software vs hardware reset effects
description: Software reset via emulator does not modify GPIO signal configuration or state. A PSC-invoked reset (clock disable, PSC reset, clock enable) restores default register settings. A hardware reset resets configuration and data registers to defaults, affecting pin state. Understanding these differences is critical for reliable recovery. Software should reapply configuration after any reset that affects GPIO.
dependencies: PSC control; System reset controller
Remarks: After PSC or hardware reset, re-run the full initialization including pin-mux and DIR settings.

feature_name: Emulation suspend behavior (halts/breakpoints)
description: The GPIO peripheral is not affected by emulation suspend events. Halting the CPU does not halt internal GPIO logic. Edges and interrupts may continue to occur while the CPU is halted. This must be considered during interactive debug sessions. Use caution to avoid missed events when resuming execution.
dependencies: Debugger/emulator; CPU halt behavior
Remarks: Mask interrupts or isolate external stimuli during halted debug to prevent queueing unexpected events.

feature_name: Industry standards compliance statement
description: The GPIO peripheral itself is not compliant to any external connectivity standards. While software may implement higher-level protocols over GPIO, the hardware provides only generic digital I/O. No protocol framing or timing enforcement is provided. Interoperability thus depends entirely on software design. Refer to specific protocol specs if implementing bit-banged interfaces.
dependencies: Software protocol stacks as applicable
Remarks: Use GPIO for simple control or for low-speed bit-banged protocols with careful timing.

feature_name: Peripheral Identification (PID) and revision reporting
description: PID exposes scheme, function code, and major/minor revision for the GPIO IP. Software can read PID to detect IP version and apply workarounds if needed. The GPIO function code is 0x483. Major and minor revisions indicate the specific implementation. This register is read-only with defined reset values.
dependencies: NONE
Remarks: Log PID on boot to aid field diagnostics and compatibility checks.

feature_name: Register map organization and naming for paired banks
description: All data, direction, trigger, and status registers are 32-bit and cover banks 0 and 1 together. Bit fields map directly to GP[0..31], with lower 16 bits for bank 0 and upper 16 for bank 1. The consistent naming suffix '01' denotes coverage of both banks. This simplifies register addressing and loops in drivers. It also eases bulk operations across multiple pins.
dependencies: NONE
Remarks: Abstract bank/bit math in helper macros to avoid off-by-one errors.

feature_name: Readback considerations: OUT_DATA vs IN_DATA
description: OUT_DATA01 readbacks reflect the register contents, not necessarily the live pin level. IN_DATA01 reflects the actual logic on the external signal and for outputs returns the driven value. For open-drain or wired-OR nets, IN_DATA01 indicates the wired result, which may differ from the output register. Use IN_DATA01 for verification of physical state. This distinction is important for validation and diagnostics.
dependencies: External pull configuration; DIR settings
Remarks: When validating hardware, always sample IN_DATA01 rather than trusting OUT_DATA01.

feature_name: Interrupt routing is not multiplexed with other functions
description: GPIO interrupts are dedicated and not multiplexed with other interrupt functions on the device. This simplifies configuration and avoids shared-vector ambiguity. It ensures predictable routing for ISR attachment. Combined with bank enables, this yields clear ownership of GPIO interrupt sources. Software still must consult device manuals for exact interrupt numbers.
dependencies: CPU interrupt controller mapping (device-specific)
Remarks: Reserve ISR vectors and document pin-to-ISR linkage clearly.

feature_name: Ability to generate interrupts from output-driven edges (for test)
description: Edge detection and interrupt/event generation do not require the pin to be configured as an input. Software can toggle an output and observe the corresponding interrupt, which is useful for connectivity tests. This enables self-test patterns without external stimulus. Care must be taken to avoid feedback into system logic. Disable after validation to prevent unintended ISRs.
dependencies: DIR settings; Edge trigger configuration; BINTEN enable
Remarks: Use this to validate ISR paths and EDMA triggers during bring-up.

feature_name: Shared enable for interrupts and EDMA events in BINTEN
description: The same BINTEN bits gate both CPU interrupt and EDMA event generation for a bank. Masking a bank thus suppresses both mechanisms. This coupling simplifies coarse control but requires coordination if only one mechanism should be active. Per-pin edge configuration still applies underneath the bank gate. Review system needs before masking banks.
dependencies: EDMA mapping; CPU interrupt controller; Edge trigger configuration
Remarks: If only EDMA is needed, ensure CPU interrupt controller also masks or ignores the source.

feature_name: Security considerations for register access
description: The specification does not describe any GPIO-specific security, privilege, or access control features. Access is memory-mapped and assumed governed by system-level protection (MMU/MPU or bus firewalls). Drivers should avoid exposing unvetted user pathways to GPIO to prevent unintended hardware control. Any authentication, sandboxing, or isolation must be implemented above this peripheral. Treat GPIO as a trusted-kernel-only resource in secure designs.
dependencies: System memory protection units; OS privilege model
Remarks: Harden user-space interfaces and validate configurations to mitigate abuse or misconfiguration risks.

feature_name: Performance considerations: module clock and concurrency
description: GPIO sampling and edge detection occur on the module clock domain at CPU/6, setting an upper bound on detectable edge rate. The separate SET/CLR registers reduce contention and avoid RMW hazards in multi-context systems. Banked 32-bit accesses allow efficient batch operations. W1C status handling reduces ISR overhead. Careful timing analysis is required for bit-banged protocols.
dependencies: PSC clock frequency; Software ISR latency; System bus bandwidth
Remarks: Use SET/CLR and banked writes to minimize latency; avoid exceeding edge detection bandwidth.
